Not automatically. Your built image includes whatever `ubuntu` resolved to at build time. If later `ubuntu:latest` changes upstream, your existing built image doesn’t change. You’d need to rebuild (and likely pull the updated base image) to incorporate updates. Typical workflow: - `docker build --pull -t myimage .` `--pull` tells Docker to check for newer versions of base images.
So images are immutable once built?
Conceptually yes: an image ID corresponds to specific content and doesn’t change. Tags are mutable pointers to images; the tag can move, but the image ID/digest is immutable.
Image ID vs tag is confusing. When I do `docker images`, I see both.

In `docker image ls`, you see: - REPOSITORY and TAG: human-friendly names (mutable pointers) - IMAGE ID: an identifier for the image (historically a truncated hash) - CREATED, SIZE The same IMAGE ID can have multiple tags (e.g., `myapp:latest` and `myapp:1.0` pointing to the same image). And tags can be retargeted.
Then containers reference images by ID?

Internally, yes. When a container is created, it references a specific image (by its immutable content). Even if you later retag `myapp:latest` to a new image, the existing container still uses the old image it was created from.
If I delete an image, what happens to containers based on it?

If a container exists (even stopped), Docker won’t let you remove the image unless you force it, because the container depends on it. You’d typically remove containers first, then remove the image: - `docker rm <container>` - `docker rmi <image>` If you force-remove an image that containers depend on, you may break things; Docker usually prevents that.
What about `docker system prune`? I’m afraid to run it.

Caution is good. `docker system prune` removes unused data: - stopped containers - unused networks - dangling images (images not referenced by any tag) - build cache By default it does not remove named volumes. If you add `--volumes`, it will remove unused volumes too, which can delete data you care about. Before pruning, you can see what would be removed with: - `docker system df` And you can run: - `docker system prune --dry-run` (supported in newer versions)
You mentioned volumes. Are volumes part of images or containers?

Volumes are separate from both. They’re managed storage that lives outside the container’s writable layer and is intended for persistent data. Key points: - Images may declare mount points with `VOLUME` instruction, but that doesn’t store data; it just marks a path as a volume location. - Containers can mount volumes at runtime (`-v` or `--mount`), and data written there persists even if the container is removed.
What’s the difference between a volume and a bind mount?

Bind mount maps a host path into the container: - `-v /home/me/data:/data` Pros: direct access to host files, easy for development. Cons: host-path-specific, permissions issues, less portable. Named volume is managed by Docker: - `-v mydata:/data` Pros: portable across hosts (as long as you migrate volume), Docker manages location, often fewer permission surprises. Cons: not as directly visible on host unless you inspect where Docker stores it. Modern syntax: - `--mount type=bind,source=...,target=...` - `--mount type=volume,source=mydata,target=...`
If I copy files into an image using `COPY`, that’s baked in, right?

Yes. `COPY` puts files into the image layer at build time. Every container created from that image starts with those files. If you later edit those files in a running container, that edit is in the container’s writable layer (unless the path is mounted as a volume/bind mount).
So for development, people mount source code as a bind mount so changes reflect immediately?

Exactly. You might run: - `docker run -it --rm -v "$PWD":/app -w /app node:20 bash` Then run your dev commands inside. The container uses Node from the image but your code comes from the host. Changes on the host show up inside instantly.
That’s cool. But doesn’t it defeat the purpose of building an image?

It depends on your goal: - For development, bind mounts give fast iteration. - For deployment or reproducible runs, you bake code into the image and run without bind mounts. A common pattern is to have a Dockerfile for production and a separate dev setup using volumes/bind mounts.
I’ve also seen `docker-compose`. Is that related to images/containers?

Docker Compose is a tool to define and run multi-container applications. It still uses images and containers; it just manages them together via a YAML file. In Compose: - `services:` define containers to run. - A service can be created from an `image:` (pull) or `build:` (build from Dockerfile). - Compose creates networks, volumes, and containers with predictable names.
If a Compose service uses `build: .`, does it build an image each time?

It builds an image when needed. If nothing changed and cache is valid, it may reuse the existing image. If you run: - `docker compose up --build` it forces a build attempt before starting. Compose typically tags the built image with a project-specific name like `myproject-web` (depending on config).
Are Compose containers different from normal Docker containers?
Same underlying container concept. Compose just orchestrates creation and naming. You can still see them with `docker ps`, inspect them, exec into them, etc.
I think the class vs instance analogy helps. But I keep thinking: if an image is a template, why is it so big? Like gigabytes sometimes.

Because the image includes a filesystem snapshot: OS libraries, language runtimes, dependencies, your app, etc. If you start from a large base image (like a full Ubuntu), you inherit all its layers. Ways to reduce size: - use smaller base images (`alpine`, `slim`, distroless) when appropriate - multi-stage builds to exclude build tools from the final image - clean up package caches in the same layer - avoid copying unnecessary files (use `.dockerignore`)
Multi-stage builds sound intimidating. Can you explain with an example?

Sure. The idea is: one stage has build tools, another stage is the minimal runtime. You copy only the build output into the final stage. Example for a Go app: Dockerfile: - Stage 1 (builder): - `FROM golang:1.22 AS builder` - `WORKDIR /src` - `COPY . .` - `RUN go build -o /out/myapp ./cmd/myapp` - Stage 2 (runtime): - `FROM gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12` - `COPY --from=builder /out/myapp /myapp` - `ENTRYPOINT ["/myapp"]` Result: final image doesn’t include Go toolchain, source code, etc. Much smaller and with fewer attack surface components.
So the final image is a different image than the builder stage?

Yes. Each stage is its own image during build. Docker only keeps the final stage as the output image (unless you explicitly target a stage). Intermediate stages may remain in build cache. You can build a specific stage with: - `docker build --target builder -t myapp-builder .`
I’ve heard of “distroless.” What does that mean?

Distroless images contain only what’s needed to run the application: usually your binary and minimal runtime libraries, without a full shell, package manager, etc. Pros: - smaller images - reduced attack surface - fewer components to patch Cons: - harder to debug interactively (no shell) A common approach is to have a debug variant or use ephemeral debug containers.
If distroless has no shell, how do you `exec` into it?

You often can’t `exec bash` because there’s no bash. Options: - Use `docker exec` to run whatever binaries exist (maybe `/busybox/sh` if included, but distroless often doesn’t). - Use a sidecar/debug container attached to the same network/volume. - Temporarily run a debug build based on a fuller image. - Use `docker run --rm -it --entrypoint sh <debug-image>` for a debug image variant.
Back to images vs containers: Is it accurate to say “images are static, containers are dynamic”?
Yes, that’s a good summary: - Image: static artifact (immutable content) - Container: dynamic runtime instance (has a lifecycle, can change state, can produce logs, can consume CPU/memory)
Where do environment variables fit? Are they in images or containers?

Both can define them, but in different ways: - In an image: `ENV FOO=bar` in Dockerfile sets a default. - At container runtime: `docker run -e FOO=baz ...` sets/overrides for that container instance. So environment variables are part of the container configuration at creation time, with defaults coming from the image config.
If I set `ENV` in Dockerfile, can I change it later without rebuilding?
You can override at runtime per container with `-e`, but you can’t change the image’s baked default without rebuilding (or creating a new image).
Is there a way to see what env vars are set in an image?

Yes: - `docker image inspect <image>` shows config including `Env`, `Entrypoint`, `Cmd`, etc. For a running container: - `docker inspect <container>` shows the container’s config (including env) and runtime details.
Sometimes I see images with `<none>` repository or tag. What are those?

Those are “dangling images.” They often occur when you rebuild an image with the same tag: the old image loses its tag and becomes untagged (dangling), but still exists until garbage collected or pruned. You can list dangling images: - `docker images -f dangling=true` And remove them: - `docker image prune`
So a tag is just a pointer, and rebuilding moves the pointer to the new image?
Exactly.
Another confusion: I heard “containers are lightweight VMs.” Is that actually true?

It’s a common simplification, but not quite. Containers are OS-level isolation, not hardware virtualization: - Containers share the host kernel. - VMs run their own kernel via a hypervisor. Containers can feel VM-like because they have isolated filesystem, process tree, network interfaces, etc., but the kernel is shared, which makes them lighter and faster to start.
Then why do some people run Docker on Mac/Windows with a VM?

Because Docker containers require a Linux kernel. On macOS and Windows, Docker Desktop runs a lightweight Linux VM behind the scenes to provide that kernel. So: - On Linux: containers run directly. - On Mac/Windows: containers run inside a Linux VM, but Docker Desktop makes it feel integrated.
Does that change anything about images vs containers?

The concepts remain the same. But it can affect: - file performance for bind mounts (host filesystem integration) - networking quirks (ports forwarded from VM) - where Docker stores its data (inside the VM) Still: image = artifact; container = instance.
I ran `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx` and it worked. Is the port mapping part of the container?

Yes, port publishing is a container runtime configuration. The `-p 8080:80` means: - host port 8080 forwards to container port 80 The image might declare `EXPOSE 80` as documentation/metadata, but that does not publish the port by itself. Publishing happens at container creation (`docker run -p` or Compose `ports:`).
So `EXPOSE` doesn’t actually open ports?

Correct. `EXPOSE` is metadata indicating the container listens on that port. It can be used by tools and by `-P` (publish all exposed ports to random host ports), but by itself it doesn’t create host port bindings.
Wait, what is `-P`?
`-P` (capital P) tells Docker to publish all exposed ports to random high ports on the host. Example: - `docker run -P nginx` Then `docker ps` will show which host ports were assigned.
Good to know. Another thing: Are containers isolated networks by default?

By default, Docker creates a bridge network (often named `bridge`) and connects containers to it unless you specify otherwise. Containers on the same bridge network can usually reach each other by IP; on user-defined bridge networks they can reach by container name too (built-in DNS). You can list networks: - `docker network ls` Inspect a network: - `docker network inspect <network>` Compose typically creates a user-defined network for the project, which is why service name DNS works.
So networking settings are container-level too?
Yes. The image doesn’t define which network it will be attached to. That’s decided when creating the container (`docker run --network ...` or Compose networks).
If I have multiple containers from the same image, can they have different ports and env vars?

Absolutely. That’s a big point of containers: same image, many differently-configured instances. Example: - `docker run -d --name web1 -p 8081:80 -e MODE=blue myweb:1.0` - `docker run -d --name web2 -p 8082:80 -e MODE=green myweb:1.0` Same image, two containers, different runtime configuration.
That’s starting to click. But I still don’t understand why people say “don’t store state in containers.” If I have a database container, isn’t that state?

The database process runs in a container, but the data directory should be on a volume. The container itself (the process + writable layer) is not the durable storage. If you store the database files in the container layer and the container is removed, you lose data. If you store them in a volume, you can remove and recreate the container while keeping the data volume.
So a typical DB run would include `-v dbdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data`?
Exactly. That’s the standard pattern.
I once deleted a container and the data was still there; I was surprised.

That’s likely because you used a named volume. Volumes persist independently of containers. Deleting a container does not delete its volumes unless you explicitly request it (e.g., `docker rm -v` for anonymous volumes; named volumes still require separate removal or prune).
Anonymous volumes?

If you mount a volume without naming it, Docker can create an anonymous volume, like: - `docker run -v /data myimage` That creates a volume with a generated name and mounts it at `/data`. They persist too, but they’re harder to track because the name is random. Compose can create anonymous volumes as well if configured that way. Named volumes are easier to manage.
If images are read-only, how do logs work? Where do container logs go?

Container logs are typically captured from stdout/stderr of the container’s main process and stored by Docker’s logging driver. Default logging driver is often `json-file`: - logs stored on the host under Docker’s data directory - `docker logs <container>` reads them You can configure other drivers (syslog, journald, fluentd, etc.). Logs are not stored in the image. They’re runtime output.
So best practice is to log to stdout, not to files inside the container?

Generally yes, for containerized apps: - Write logs to stdout/stderr so the platform can collect and manage them. - If you write logs to files inside the container filesystem, you risk losing them when the container is removed, and they can bloat the writable layer. There are cases for file logs, but you’d typically use a volume or sidecar log collector.
You mentioned `.dockerignore` earlier. Is that like `.gitignore`?

Very similar. `.dockerignore` tells Docker which files to exclude from the build context (the set of files sent to the Docker daemon during `docker build`). This matters because: - sending huge contexts is slow - it can leak secrets accidentally (e.g., `.env`, private keys) - it can break caching (changes in irrelevant files invalidate layers) Common entries: - `node_modules` - `.git` - build artifacts - secrets files
Wait, “sent to the Docker daemon”? Isn’t Docker running locally?

Often yes, but Docker uses a client-server model: - The `docker` CLI is the client. - The Docker daemon (`dockerd`) is the server that builds images and runs containers. Even on your own machine, the CLI sends the build context to the daemon. On Docker Desktop, the daemon is inside the VM, so the context is transferred into that VM.
So if I have a big folder, it can take ages just to “send context.”
Exactly. You’ll see output like: - “Sending build context to Docker daemon  1.2GB” That’s a sign you should use `.dockerignore` and structure your build context carefully.
Is BuildKit related to this?

BuildKit is Docker’s modern build engine. It improves performance, caching, parallelism, and features like secret mounts and SSH forwarding during build. You’ll often see BuildKit style output with numbered steps and progress bars. You can enable it via environment variable (often default now): - `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build .`
Secret mounts? That sounds useful. I was worried about putting tokens in images.

You should avoid baking secrets into images. BuildKit provides mechanisms to use secrets during build without storing them in the final image layers. For example, you can mount a secret file during a `RUN` step: - `RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mysecret ...` And pass it at build time: - `docker build --secret id=mysecret,src=path/to/secret .` This way, the secret is available only during that build step and not committed into layers.
Does that mean the image won’t contain the secret even if a command uses it?

Correct, as long as you don’t write the secret into the filesystem in a way that persists into the layer. The secret mount is ephemeral for that step. But you still need to be careful: if your command outputs the secret into logs or writes it to a file that becomes part of the layer, it could leak.
Got it. Another beginner question: What is `docker save` and `docker load`?

Those are for exporting/importing images as tar archives: - `docker save -o image.tar myimage:tag` exports one or more images (including layers and metadata). - `docker load -i image.tar` imports them into another Docker daemon. This is different from `docker export`/`docker import` which deal with containers and filesystem snapshots (and can lose metadata like history/layers).
So `docker save` preserves layers and tags?
It preserves layers and metadata; tags can be preserved depending on what you save. If you save `myimage:tag`, when you load it you’ll typically get that repo:tag again.
What about `docker export`?

`docker export <container>` exports a container’s filesystem as a tar (flattened), without image history and without the layered structure. If you `docker import` that tar, you get a new image with a single layer and limited metadata. It’s useful for some cases, but not common for standard workflows.
So images are layered; export/import flattens.
Exactly.
Are layers always shared between images?

Layers can be shared if they have identical content. That’s a big space-saving feature. If two images share a base image, they reuse the same base layers on disk. That’s why pulling multiple images based on the same distro doesn’t multiply disk usage linearly.
If I have two tags pointing to the same image, that’s also shared obviously.
Yes—same image ID means same layers.
What is a “manifest” in Docker images?

In registries, an image reference like `nginx:1.27` often points to a manifest, which describes the image’s layers and config. For multi-architecture images, the tag can point to a manifest list (index) that references different manifests for different platforms (amd64, arm64, etc.). When you pull, Docker selects the appropriate one for your platform.
So `nginx` can be multi-arch and I don’t have to think about it?

Usually yes. But if you’re on Apple Silicon (arm64) and you pull an image that’s only amd64, Docker might use emulation (like QEMU) or fail depending on setup. You can specify platform: - `docker pull --platform linux/amd64 nginx:...` - `docker run --platform linux/amd64 ...`
That’s helpful. I ran into that once and didn’t understand why it was slow.
Emulation can be slower, yes.
Coming back to containers: If a container is just a process, why does it have an IP address?

Because Docker sets up network namespaces for the container. The container gets its own virtual network interface, its own IP within the Docker network, and its own routing table (within that namespace). It’s still just processes, but the OS provides isolation for networking.
Is that also why containers can run the same port internally?
Exactly. Two containers can both listen on port 80 internally because they have separate network namespaces. The host port mapping decides how (and if) you expose those ports to the host.
So to avoid conflicts, I just map to different host ports.
Right: - Container A: `-p 8081:80` - Container B: `-p 8082:80`
If I don’t map ports, can I still access the service?

You can access it from other containers on the same Docker network using the container IP or name (on user-defined networks). From the host, not directly (unless you use host networking or special routing). On Linux, you might reach container IP on the bridge, but it’s not the standard approach. Publishing ports is the typical way to make it accessible from the host.
Host networking?

`--network host` makes the container share the host network namespace. Then the container listens directly on the host’s interfaces (no NAT, no separate IP). This can improve performance or simplify certain network setups, but it removes network isolation and can cause port conflicts. It behaves differently on Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows), where “host network” doesn’t work the same due to the VM layer.
That’s a lot. I think I’m getting the core concept though. Can I try to restate it and you correct me?
Yes—go for it.

An image is like a read-only snapshot with layers, built from a Dockerfile or pulled from a registry. A container is created from an image and has its own runtime config like env vars, port mappings, networks, and a writable layer for changes. If I remove the container, the writable changes go away unless I used volumes.
That’s accurate. The only tweak I’d add: the image is not just “a snapshot” but also includes configuration metadata (default command, env, etc.). But you already implied that.
Nice. Then what is an “image layer” exactly? Is it like a diff?

Yes. Each layer is essentially a set of filesystem changes (add/modify/delete) relative to the previous layer. Docker uses a union filesystem (like overlayfs) to present these layers as a single merged filesystem. When you run a container, Docker adds a writable layer on top, so reads come from the merged view and writes go to the top layer.
How do deletes work in layers? If a file existed in a lower layer and I delete it in a higher layer, is it gone?

It’s “masked” using a concept called a whiteout. The file still exists in the lower layer, but the higher layer records that it should be considered deleted in the merged view. This is why deleting big files in a later layer doesn’t reduce image size: the bytes are still present in the lower layer and must be downloaded/stored. To avoid that, you want to avoid adding the big files in the first place, or ensure they never land in a committed layer (e.g., do download + use + cleanup in the same `RUN` step if possible).
That’s a really important point. So if I accidentally `COPY` a huge file and then delete it in the next step, the image is still huge.
Exactly.
Is that also why people say “combine commands in one RUN”?

Partly. Combining commands helps ensure temporary files don’t get stuck in earlier layers. But you should balance that with readability. Multi-stage builds are often a cleaner way to keep final images small.
How do I see what’s taking space in an image?

A few tools: - `docker history <image>` shows layer sizes per instruction. - `docker image inspect <image>` shows metadata and layer digests. - Third-party tools like `dive` can analyze image content and show which files contribute to each layer.
Another thing: I see `COPY package.json package-lock.json ./` then `RUN npm ci` then `COPY . .` in Node Dockerfiles. Why that order?

That’s about caching. Dependencies change less frequently than application source code. By copying only `package*.json` first and running `npm ci`, Docker can cache the dependency install layer. Then when you change your app code and rebuild, Docker can reuse the dependency layer and only redo the final `COPY . .` and subsequent steps. If you copied all source code before installing dependencies, any code change would invalidate the cache and force reinstalling dependencies, which is slow.
That’s clever. So build cache depends on the contents copied.
Right. Docker cache is sensitive to: - the Dockerfile instruction text - the files in the build context that are referenced by that step (for COPY/ADD) - prior layers
You said earlier not all instructions create filesystem layers. Does that matter?

It matters mostly for understanding caching and size. Metadata instructions still create a new image “config” step, but not a filesystem diff. `docker history` will show them as 0B sometimes. But they can still invalidate cache for later steps because they are part of the build sequence.
What about `ARG`?

`ARG` defines build-time variables. They can affect caching because if an `ARG` value changes and it’s used in a step, that step’s cache may be invalidated. `ARG` differs from `ENV`: - `ARG` is available during build only (unless you copy it into ENV). - `ENV` persists in the image and is available at runtime.
If I do `ARG VERSION` and then `RUN echo $VERSION`, does the output get baked into the layer?

The command output isn’t stored as a file unless you redirect it, but the layer’s cache key depends on the command and build args. If you use `VERSION` to download something or write files, then yes, it affects the layer content. Also, the image history may show the command, which can leak values if you put secrets into ARG (don’t do that).
So ARG is not for secrets either.
Correct—ARG values can show up in build logs and image history. For secrets, use BuildKit secrets.
Now, containers: what is the difference between `docker create` and `docker run`?

`docker create` creates a container but doesn’t start it. It’s like “instantiate the container” from the image. - `docker create --name myc nginx` Then: - `docker start myc` `docker run` does create + start in one step.
When I run `docker run -it ubuntu`, it drops me into a shell and then exits when I type `exit`. Is the container gone?

Not unless you used `--rm`. Without `--rm`, the container stops but still exists (in exited state). You can see it with `docker ps -a` and start it again (though starting it will run the default command again; for ubuntu that might be `bash` if you started it interactively). If you want it to be removed automatically, add `--rm`.
But if it’s stopped, it’s still using disk space because of its writable layer?
Yes. Stopped containers still occupy disk space (their writable layer and metadata). That’s why pruning stopped containers can recover space.
Is it okay to have lots of stopped containers?
It’s fine until it isn’t—mostly a disk usage and clutter issue. For tidy systems, periodically remove unused containers/images/volumes carefully.
What’s the difference between removing a container and removing an image?

Removing a container deletes that instance (including its writable layer). Removing an image deletes the shared read-only layers (if nothing else references them). You can remove containers without removing images; images can be reused to create new containers.
So if I want to “reset” a container, I should remove and recreate it, not try to clean it manually.
Exactly. That’s the immutable infrastructure mindset.
How does this relate to versioning? Like if I have `myapp:1.0` image and I update the app, do I build `myapp:1.1` and run new containers?

That’s a common pattern: - Build a new image tag for each version. - Deploy by creating new containers from the new image. - Remove old containers when done. It makes rollbacks easier: you can run containers from the previous image tag.
But tags can be overwritten, right? Like I could rebuild `myapp:1.0` and push it again.

You can, but it’s considered bad practice for version tags. Better: - treat version tags as immutable - allow `latest` (or a moving tag like `stable`) to move - optionally use digests for strict immutability
Speaking of push: `docker push` pushes images, not containers?

Correct. Registries store images. Containers are runtime instances and aren’t pushed. If you want to share what’s running, you’d build an image (Dockerfile) or commit a container to an image (less ideal) and push that image.
If I commit a container, does it include volumes?
No. Volumes are outside the container filesystem. `docker commit` captures the container’s writable layer changes, not external volumes.
That’s good. Another question: what’s inside an image besides files? Like is there a list of layers?

An image includes: - a config JSON (entrypoint, cmd, env, working dir, labels, etc.) - a list of layer digests (filesystem diffs) - optionally, history metadata (commands used to create layers) In registries, there are manifests that reference these blobs.
Can I inspect the JSON?
`docker image inspect` prints JSON including config. For lower-level inspection of registry blobs, there are tools like `skopeo` or `crane`, but for beginners `docker inspect` is enough.
I’m curious about `LABEL`. Is that just metadata?

Yes. Labels are key-value metadata you can attach to images and containers. Uses: - add version/build info - annotate with source repo URL - mark maintainer - help with automation (filtering) Example: - `LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="..."` You can filter images/containers by label: - `docker ps --filter label=...` - `docker images --filter label=...`
Are labels inherited from image to container?

Image labels are on the image. Containers can also have labels set at creation. Some tooling uses image labels; others use container labels. They’re separate, though you can set similar values in both.
What about “OCI”? I see that term.

OCI is the Open Container Initiative. It defines standards for: - image format (OCI Image Spec) - runtime (OCI Runtime Spec) Docker images are effectively compatible with OCI images. Many tools now use OCI standards, and runtimes like `containerd` and `runc` implement them.
So Docker isn’t the only container system.
Right. Docker popularized the experience, but the ecosystem includes other tools and runtimes.
If containers share the host kernel, does that mean I can’t run Windows containers on Linux?

Correct. You generally need a compatible kernel/OS. Windows containers require Windows kernel. On Windows you can run Linux containers via a Linux VM (WSL2/Docker Desktop) and Windows containers natively, but you typically switch modes.
Back to basics: how do I know if I’m looking at an image or a container when I see an ID?

Good question. Image IDs and container IDs are different namespaces, but both are hex-like strings. The command context matters: - `docker image ls` shows image IDs - `docker ps` shows container IDs To be sure: - `docker inspect <id>` works for both; Docker will interpret it. If ambiguous, you can specify `docker image inspect` vs `docker container inspect`.
I’ve seen `docker container ls` too.

Yes. Docker has management commands grouped: - `docker container ...` - `docker image ...` - `docker network ...` - `docker volume ...` They map to older shorthand: - `docker ps` = `docker container ls` - `docker images` = `docker image ls` etc.
That’s probably better for clarity.
Agreed, especially when learning.
Can we walk through a concrete example? Like build an image and run two containers from it.

Sure. Example: a tiny Python web server. 1) Create `app.py`: - a simple HTTP server on port 8000 2) Dockerfile: - `FROM python:3.12-slim` - `WORKDIR /app` - `COPY app.py .` - `EXPOSE 8000` - `CMD ["python", "app.py"]` Build: - `docker build -t mypyserver:1.0 .` Run two containers: - `docker run -d --name s1 -p 8001:8000 mypyserver:1.0` - `docker run -d --name s2 -p 8002:8000 mypyserver:1.0` Now: - same image `mypyserver:1.0` - two different containers `s1` and `s2` - different host port mappings
If I `curl localhost:8001` and `curl localhost:8002`, I should get responses from each container.

Exactly. If your server prints something different based on env var, you can set: - `docker run -d --name s1 -p 8001:8000 -e INSTANCE=one mypyserver:1.0` - `docker run -d --name s2 -p 8002:8000 -e INSTANCE=two mypyserver:1.0` Same image, different runtime behavior.
If I change `app.py` on my host and rebuild the image, do the running containers change?

No. Running containers keep using the filesystem they started with. If you rebuild and get a new image, you need to create new containers from it (or recreate existing ones). This is another key image vs container distinction: containers do not magically update when images change.
So to update, I stop and remove old containers and run new ones.
Exactly. Or in Compose, you do `docker compose up -d --build` which recreates containers when the image changes.
Recreate means remove and create again?
Yes. Compose will typically stop the old container and create a new one with the updated image (depending on settings). It tries to be safe and keep volumes.
What about `docker restart`? That doesn’t pick up new image changes, right?
Correct. Restart just restarts the same container instance.
I think I finally see why these are separate concepts. Image is like “the package,” container is like “the running program.”
That’s a great phrasing.
Last confusion: sometimes I see people say “build a container.” But you build an image, right?
Right. People often say “build a container” colloquially, but technically you build an image and run a container. The build output is an image.
That clears it up. Can we talk about `FROM scratch`? Is that a real image?

`scratch` is a special empty base image. It’s used for building minimal images, typically for statically linked binaries (like Go) where you don’t need OS libraries. Example: - build a static binary - `FROM scratch` - `COPY mybinary /mybinary` - `ENTRYPOINT ["/mybinary"]` There’s no shell, no package manager, nothing else.
If there’s nothing, how does SSL certificates work?

Good catch. If your app needs CA certificates to make HTTPS requests, you need to include them. That’s why people often use: - distroless base images that include certs - or manually copy certs into the scratch image - or use `alpine` and install `ca-certificates` (though that adds more)
So super minimal images can be tricky.
Yes. Minimalism is good, but only if it still meets runtime requirements.
Thanks. I think I’m good now. But I might ask again if I get confused.
Anytime—if you describe what command you ran and what you expected, it’s usually easy to pinpoint whether it’s an image vs container issue (or volumes, or caching).
I keep hearing about “container runtime” like containerd and runc. Where does Docker fit into that? Is Docker the runtime?

Docker (the full Docker Engine) includes multiple pieces. Historically it did “everything,” but under the hood on most modern setups: - containerd is the higher-level runtime that manages images, containers, snapshots, and lifecycle. - runc is the low-level OCI runtime that actually creates the container process (sets up namespaces/cgroups and execs the process). - Docker Engine (dockerd) is the API server and orchestration layer that users interact with via the Docker CLI; it talks to containerd. So: Docker is not just “the runtime,” but it uses runtimes. When you say “a container runtime,” you’re usually referring to containerd/runc (or alternatives), not the Docker CLI itself.
Does that matter day-to-day, or is it just trivia?

Mostly conceptual, unless you’re debugging low-level behavior, using Kubernetes, or working in environments that don’t use Docker Engine. Kubernetes commonly uses containerd directly (not dockerd). But the image/container concepts stay basically the same because they’re standardized (OCI).
You mentioned namespaces and cgroups a few times. Can you explain what isolation a container actually gets?

A container is a regular Linux process with isolation features: - Namespaces: isolate “views” of system resources. - PID namespace: container has its own process tree (its own PID 1, etc.). - Mount namespace: its own filesystem mount table. - Network namespace: its own interfaces, IPs, routing, ports. - UTS namespace: hostname/domain isolation. - IPC namespace: shared memory, semaphores isolation. - User namespace (optional): maps container user IDs to different host IDs. - cgroups: limit and account for resources. - CPU limits/shares, memory limits, pids limit, block I/O constraints, etc. This is why containers feel like “mini-systems” without being VMs.
If containers are just processes, can they see host processes?

With PID namespaces (the default), a container sees only processes in its namespace. Without that isolation (or with special flags), it could see more. Also, privileged containers or host PID namespace (`--pid=host`) changes that. But default: no, it won’t see host processes.
What does `--privileged` actually do? People warn about it.

`--privileged` essentially removes many safety barriers: - grants broad Linux capabilities - allows access to all devices - relaxes seccomp/AppArmor restrictions (depending on config) - makes it much easier to break out or affect the host It’s sometimes used for low-level tools (like Docker-in-Docker or hardware access), but it’s generally a last resort. Prefer adding specific capabilities (`--cap-add`) or device mounts instead.
Capabilities are like partial root privileges?

Exactly. Linux splits “root power” into discrete capabilities (e.g., `NET_ADMIN`, `SYS_PTRACE`). Containers typically run with a reduced set. If something needs a specific privileged operation, you can add just that capability rather than going full privileged.
How does user identity work inside containers? If I run as root in the container, am I root on the host?

By default on Linux, container root maps to host root (UID 0), but it’s constrained by namespaces/cgroups and security profiles. Still, running as root in containers increases risk, because misconfigurations or kernel vulnerabilities can lead to host impact. If user namespaces are enabled, container root can map to an unprivileged UID on the host, which improves safety. Many setups don’t enable this by default, but it’s becoming more common.
So best practice is to run as a non-root user in the container?

Yes, when feasible: - In Dockerfile: create a user and `USER appuser` - Also ensure the app doesn’t require privileged ports (<1024) or root-only file paths. It won’t solve everything, but it reduces the blast radius.
I’ve seen `USER 1000:1000` without creating the user. Is that okay?

It can be. Linux doesn’t require the user to exist in `/etc/passwd` for the kernel to run a process with that UID/GID. Some software expects a username entry, though. If you see odd behavior (like tools complaining about “unknown user”), create the user explicitly.
Changing gears: what’s the difference between a volume and a “tmpfs mount”?

A tmpfs mount stores data in memory (RAM) instead of disk. In Docker: - `--mount type=tmpfs,target=/tmp` (or `--tmpfs /tmp`) Pros: - fast - data disappears when container stops - avoids writing sensitive temp data to disk Cons: - uses memory - not persistent Volumes/bind mounts are disk-backed and persistent (unless you delete them).
That seems useful for secrets too.

It can be helpful for runtime secrets if you mount them from a secret manager or file, but the bigger “don’t bake secrets into images” point still stands. For build-time secrets, use BuildKit secrets. For runtime secrets, use orchestrator-managed secrets (Swarm/Kubernetes) or carefully mounted files.
What about `.env` files in Compose? Are those secrets?

They can contain secrets, but Compose `.env` is just a convenience mechanism to substitute variables into the Compose file and/or provide environment variables. It’s not encrypted or protected. Treat it like sensitive plaintext: don’t commit it to git, and don’t assume it’s safe just because it’s “.env”.
Another concept I hear: “healthchecks.” Are those part of the image or container?

They can be defined in either place: - In Dockerfile: `HEALTHCHECK ...` becomes image metadata (default healthcheck). - At runtime/Compose: you can define/override healthcheck per service/container. A healthcheck is a command Docker runs periodically inside the container. It marks the container as healthy/unhealthy (shown in `docker ps`). It doesn’t automatically restart containers by itself in plain Docker; restart behavior depends on restart policy or orchestrator.
So if my container is “unhealthy,” Docker doesn’t automatically restart it?
Not by default in standalone Docker. Restart policies (`--restart`) react to container exit, not health status. Some orchestrators can use health status to replace containers.
Restart policies confuse me. What are the common ones?

In `docker run --restart ...`: - `no` (default): don’t restart automatically - `on-failure[:max-retries]`: restart if the process exits non-zero - `always`: restart whenever it stops - `unless-stopped`: like always, but won’t restart if you manually stopped it This is container-level config, not image-level.
If my app crashes and exits 0 by mistake, on-failure won’t restart it.

Correct. Exit codes matter. Many apps should exit non-zero on crash. If you can’t guarantee that, `always` might be safer, but it can also create restart loops. Ideally fix the app’s exit behavior and add proper health checks.
Speaking of loops: what does it mean when people say “one process per container”? Is that a rule?

It’s a guideline, not a strict rule. The idea: - A container’s lifecycle is tied to its main process. - Keeping one main responsibility per container makes scaling, logging, and failure handling simpler. But you can run multiple processes if you have a good reason (e.g., a process supervisor, or tightly coupled side processes). If you do, be mindful of PID 1 issues and signal handling.
How does Docker decide when a container is “stopped”?

When the container’s main process (PID 1 inside container namespace) exits, the container is stopped. Any other processes in that container should also end, but if you have weird process trees, you can get surprises. Still, Docker treats container life = main process life.
If I `docker exec` a shell and then the main process exits, what happens to my exec session?
Usually it will be terminated because the container is stopping; the namespaces are torn down. `exec` processes live inside the container context, so if the container ends, they end too.
Another term: “sidecar container” you mentioned earlier. That’s a Kubernetes thing?

Mostly used in Kubernetes, but the concept exists generally: a separate container that supports the main container (logging agent, proxy, metrics collector). In plain Docker Compose you can do something similar by running two services and sharing a network/volume, but Compose doesn’t have the same “pod” abstraction as Kubernetes.
In Compose, containers can talk via service names. Is that DNS always available?

On user-defined networks (which Compose uses by default), yes: Docker runs an internal DNS server and assigns names/aliases. On the default `bridge` network, name resolution is more limited unless you use `--link` (legacy) or create your own network.
Are there gotchas with container names vs service names?

Yes: - In Compose, the DNS name you typically use is the service name (e.g., `db`), not the container name. - Compose container names include project prefixes (like `myproj-db-1`) unless you set `container_name` (which has tradeoffs). - Scaling a service (`--scale`) works better if you don’t hardcode `container_name`, because you’d otherwise get naming conflicts.
So inside the app container, I should connect to `db:5432` not `localhost`.
Exactly. `localhost` inside a container means “this container.” To reach another container, use its service name on the shared network.
I’ve made that mistake so many times.
It’s one of the most common container networking mistakes.
What about “localhost” from the host to reach a container?

From the host, you usually reach containers via published ports (`-p hostPort:containerPort`). Then you use `localhost:hostPort` on the host. If you didn’t publish a port, `localhost` on the host won’t magically reach it.
On Docker Desktop there’s something like `host.docker.internal`. What is that?

That’s a special DNS name Docker Desktop provides so containers can reach the host machine. It resolves to the host from inside the container environment. Useful when your container needs to call a service running on your laptop (database, API, etc.). Behavior is platform-dependent; on Linux it may require extra configuration, but on Docker Desktop it’s usually built-in.
Another thing: what are “build contexts” in Compose? If I do `build: .`, is that the context?

Yes. `build: .` means the build context is the current directory, and Compose sends that directory (minus `.dockerignore`) to the builder. You can also specify: - `build: { context: ., dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev }` - or different contexts per service.
Can I have multiple Dockerfiles for the same app?
Yes. Common patterns: - `Dockerfile` for production - `Dockerfile.dev` for development tooling Or one Dockerfile with multi-stage targets and you select a target.
How do you select a target in Compose?
In Compose build config you can set `target:`: - `build: { context: ., target: dev }` Then the Dockerfile must have `FROM ... AS dev` stage.
I’m also confused about `.dockerignore` vs `.gitignore`. If something is ignored by git, is it automatically ignored by docker build?
No. Docker only uses `.dockerignore`. Many projects keep them aligned, but you have to maintain `.dockerignore` explicitly.
Okay. Now: storage drivers. You said overlay2. Does that affect performance?

Yes. The storage driver impacts: - layer sharing behavior - copy-on-write performance - filesystem semantics and performance On Linux, overlay2 is generally the recommended default. On Docker Desktop, there’s an additional virtualization layer, so bind mount performance can be a bigger bottleneck than overlay2 itself.
If I write a lot to the container filesystem, is that slower than writing to a volume?

Often yes. Writing to the container writable layer uses copy-on-write semantics and can be slower, and it grows the container layer. Volumes are typically more direct and can be faster and more appropriate for data.
What is “copy-on-write” in this context?

When a file exists in a lower read-only layer and you modify it in a container, Docker can’t change the lower layer. Instead it copies the file up into the writable layer and applies changes there. That copy step can be expensive for large files. If your workload modifies lots of preexisting files from the image, you can pay a performance penalty.
So putting frequently changing data in volumes avoids that.
Exactly.
Can I limit container resources like memory and CPU?

Yes, with `docker run` flags: - Memory: `--memory 512m` (and optionally `--memory-swap`) - CPU: `--cpus 1.5` or `--cpu-shares`, `--cpuset-cpus` - PIDs: `--pids-limit 200` These are runtime settings (container-level). They rely on cgroups.
What happens if a container hits the memory limit?

It can be OOM-killed (out-of-memory). You might see the container exit, often with exit code 137, and logs indicating it was killed. It’s important to set sane limits and also ensure your application handles memory usage appropriately.
Are resource limits supported the same on Docker Desktop?

Mostly, but remember Docker Desktop runs inside a VM with its own resource limits. If the VM itself is limited to, say, 4GB RAM, your containers collectively can’t exceed that. So you have two levels: VM allocation and per-container limits.
Another conceptual thing: what’s the difference between `ADD` and `COPY` in Dockerfile?

Prefer `COPY` for simple copying. `ADD` has extra behaviors: - It can fetch URLs (though not recommended for reproducibility/security). - It can automatically extract local tar archives into the image. Because `ADD` does more, `COPY` is clearer and more predictable. Use `ADD` only when you specifically want those extra features (like auto-extracting a tarball from the build context).
If I use `ADD` with a URL, is it cached?

Caching can be tricky because the URL contents can change without the Dockerfile changing, so you can get stale cache or non-reproducible builds. Better to use `curl`/`wget` in a `RUN` step with pinned checksums, or download artifacts outside the build and `COPY` them in.
Speaking of checksums, how do people ensure base images are secure?

Common practices: - Pin by digest (`FROM ubuntu@sha256:...`) for reproducibility. - Use trusted registries and official images where possible. - Scan images for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Grype, Docker Scout). - Keep base images updated (rebuild regularly). - Minimize attack surface (slim/distroless, remove compilers/tools from runtime images).
If I pin by digest, how do I get security updates?

You’d update the digest intentionally when you want to consume updates. That’s the tradeoff: reproducibility vs automatic updates. Many teams automate this with dependency update bots that open PRs when new digests are available.
That makes sense. Last thing for now: I saw `docker compose down -v` and got scared. What exactly does it delete?

`docker compose down` stops and removes the Compose-managed containers and networks. With `-v`, it also removes volumes declared in the Compose file (and sometimes anonymous volumes attached to containers). If those volumes hold database data, that data is gone. So: - `down` = remove containers/network (usually safe-ish) - `down -v` = also delete volumes (data-destructive) If you want to reset just containers but keep DB data, avoid `-v`.
That answers my fear. I think I’m going to experiment more with volumes and healthchecks next.

If you tell me what stack you’re trying (e.g., app + Postgres + Redis), I can suggest a small Compose file that demonstrates: named volumes for persistence, healthchecks, and how service-to-service networking works.
I keep getting a RecursionError in my tree traversal. Can you help me figure out why?

Yes. A RecursionError in a tree traversal usually means one of these is happening: (1) the structure isn’t actually a tree (it has a cycle), (2) your traversal revisits nodes without a visited guard, (3) your base case is wrong or never reached, or (4) the tree is extremely deep (valid but deeper than Python’s recursion limit). Can you paste the traversal code and a minimal example of the node structure (class/dict) plus how you build the tree?

Sure. Here’s a simplified version. I have nodes like this: class Node: def __init__(self, value): self.value = value self.children = [] def add_child(parent, child): parent.children.append(child) And traversal: def dfs(node): print(node.value) for child in node.children: dfs(child) Then I build it: a = Node("a") b = Node("b") c = Node("c") d = Node("d") add_child(a, b) add_child(b, c) add_child(c, d) add_child(d, b)  # this is probably wrong? dfs(a) It blows up.

That last line creates a cycle: d -> b, while b is already an ancestor of d (a -> b -> c -> d). Your `dfs` assumes an acyclic tree, so it keeps going: a, b, c, d, b, c, d, b... until recursion depth is exceeded. You have two options depending on intent: 1) If it’s supposed to be a tree, fix construction: remove the cyclic edge. 2) If it can be a general graph, modify DFS to track visited nodes (by identity) to avoid revisiting: def dfs(node, visited=None): if visited is None: visited = set() if id(node) in visited: return visited.add(id(node)) print(node.value) for child in node.children: dfs(child, visited) Alternatively store nodes directly in the set by defining `__hash__` properly or relying on object identity (default objects are hashable unless you override eq). In your Node class, since you didn’t define `__eq__`, instances are hashable by identity already, so you can do `visited.add(node)`. Do you expect cycles in your data, or is that edge a bug?

In my real data it’s not supposed to have cycles, but it’s built from some references and I guess cycles can sneak in. I tried adding visited like you said but I got “TypeError: unhashable type: 'Node'” when I do visited.add(node).

That means in your real code your `Node` class likely defines `__eq__` (or is a dataclass with `eq=True`) without defining a compatible `__hash__`, which makes instances unhashable. Two quick fixes: A) Track by identity explicitly: visited.add(id(node)) ... if id(node) in visited: return B) Make Node hashable by identity: - If it’s a dataclass: `@dataclass(eq=False)` (so it keeps identity-based hash), or `@dataclass(unsafe_hash=True)` (careful; hash then depends on fields unless configured). - If it’s a custom class with __eq__: either remove/adjust __eq__ or define `__hash__ = object.__hash__` to make it hash by identity. Option A is simplest and safest when you just want cycle breaking. Can you share your real Node definition (or dataclass) and how equality is defined?

Yeah, it’s a dataclass: from dataclasses import dataclass, field @dataclass class Node: value: str children: list["Node"] = field(default_factory=list) I also compare nodes by value sometimes, so I rely on dataclass eq.

With `@dataclass` default settings, `eq=True` and `frozen=False`, Python sets `__hash__ = None`, making it unhashable. That’s why `set()` rejects it. Given you want to compare nodes by value sometimes, you have to decide what “visited” means: - If you want to avoid revisiting exact objects (cycle detection by identity), use `id(node)` in visited. That won’t conflate two different nodes with the same value. - If you want to avoid revisiting same logical node by value, use `node.value` (only if unique) or a stable key. For cycle safety, identity is usually correct. So: def dfs(node, visited=None): if visited is None: visited = set() ident = id(node) if ident in visited: return visited.add(ident) print(node.value) for child in node.children: dfs(child, visited) If you also want to detect/report cycles, you can track a recursion stack set too. Would you like it to raise an error on cycles rather than silently skip?
Yes, I’d rather detect and throw with some info about the cycle path. Skipping could hide bugs.

Use two sets: `visiting` for the current recursion stack, and `visited` for fully processed nodes. When you encounter a node already in `visiting`, you found a back-edge (cycle). To report the path, keep a parent map or an explicit stack list. Here’s a version using identity and a stack: class CycleError(Exception): pass def dfs_detect_cycle(node): visited = set() visiting = set() stack = [] def _dfs(n): ident = id(n) if ident in visiting: # Extract cycle path from stack # Find first occurrence of this node in stack idx = next(i for i, x in enumerate(stack) if id(x) == ident) cycle_nodes = stack[idx:] + [n] cycle_vals = " -> ".join(x.value for x in cycle_nodes) raise CycleError(f"Cycle detected: {cycle_vals}") if ident in visited: return visiting.add(ident) stack.append(n) for child in n.children: _dfs(child) stack.pop() visiting.remove(ident) visited.add(ident) _dfs(node) This doesn’t print values; it just validates. If you want traversal plus detection, you can print before recursing. Note: `next(i for i, x in enumerate(stack) if id(x) == ident)` assumes the node is in stack when detected; it is, because it’s in `visiting`. Try it on your sample with d->b and you should get something like: a->b->c->d->b (depending on where detected). Does your graph have shared subtrees (DAG) too? If yes, `visited` avoids re-walking them.

It does have shared subtrees. But I actually want to traverse all paths, not just unique nodes, except I want to fail on actual cycles. If a node appears under two parents I still want to visit it twice along different paths.

Then you should not use a global `visited` set, because that suppresses revisits that are legitimate for “all paths.” You only need `visiting` (recursion stack) to detect cycles. That will allow revisiting nodes from different branches as long as you’re not re-entering a node already on the current path. So: def dfs_all_paths_cycle_check(node): visiting = set() stack = [] def _dfs(n): ident = id(n) if ident in visiting: idx = next(i for i, x in enumerate(stack) if id(x) == ident) cycle_nodes = stack[idx:] + [n] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(x.value for x in cycle_nodes)) visiting.add(ident) stack.append(n) print(n.value)  # or yield n for child in n.children: _dfs(child) stack.pop() visiting.remove(ident) _dfs(node) This will print nodes multiple times if reached via different parents, which matches “all paths” semantics. If you want to collect paths rather than print nodes, you can yield or append current stack. Do you want preorder, postorder, or path enumeration?

Ultimately I want to enumerate root-to-leaf paths as lists of values. My current recursion tries to build a list, but I think I messed up and it also triggers recursion error sometimes. Here’s what I tried: def paths(node, path=[]): path.append(node.value) if not node.children: return [path] res = [] for child in node.children: res.extend(paths(child, path)) path.pop() return res This gives weird results even when there’s no cycle.

Two issues: 1) The default argument `path=[]` is shared across calls. That causes paths to accumulate across separate invocations. 2) You’re returning `[path]` which is the same list object reused; later pops mutate it, so stored paths change. Fix by using `None` default and copying when you record a path: def paths(node, path=None): if path is None: path = [] path.append(node.value) if not node.children: out = [path.copy()] else: out = [] for child in node.children: out.extend(paths(child, path)) path.pop() return out Now each leaf gets a snapshot copy. To add cycle detection for “all paths” semantics, add a `visiting` set keyed by id: def paths(node, path=None, visiting=None, stack_nodes=None): if path is None: path = [] if visiting is None: visiting = set() if stack_nodes is None: stack_nodes = [] ident = id(node) if ident in visiting: idx = next(i for i, x in enumerate(stack_nodes) if id(x) == ident) cycle = stack_nodes[idx:] + [node] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(n.value for n in cycle)) visiting.add(ident) stack_nodes.append(node) path.append(node.value) if not node.children: out = [path.copy()] else: out = [] for child in node.children: out.extend(paths(child, path, visiting, stack_nodes)) path.pop() stack_nodes.pop() visiting.remove(ident) return out This will visit shared nodes multiple times along different paths, but still fails if a node is revisited along the current recursion stack.
That makes sense. But I’m worried about performance and memory with copying paths at each leaf. In a big tree it’s huge. Is there a generator way?

Yes—yield paths lazily. You still need to create a list (or tuple) per yielded path, but you avoid storing all paths at once. Generator version: def iter_paths(node, path=None, visiting=None, stack_nodes=None): if path is None: path = [] if visiting is None: visiting = set() if stack_nodes is None: stack_nodes = [] ident = id(node) if ident in visiting: idx = next(i for i, x in enumerate(stack_nodes) if id(x) == ident) cycle = stack_nodes[idx:] + [node] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(n.value for n in cycle)) visiting.add(ident) stack_nodes.append(node) path.append(node.value) try: if not node.children: yield tuple(path)  # tuple is immutable snapshot else: for child in node.children: yield from iter_paths(child, path, visiting, stack_nodes) finally: path.pop() stack_nodes.pop() visiting.remove(ident) Using `try/finally` ensures cleanup even if an exception occurs mid-traversal. Then you can do: for p in iter_paths(root): ... If you truly have a combinatorial explosion of paths, the number of yielded paths itself is enormous—no algorithm avoids that if you must enumerate them.

I tried yield from but I got confused about where to put the pops; I think I caused “pop from empty list” once. Your try/finally pattern is nice. But I still sometimes get RecursionError even without cycles because the depth can be like 3000. How do I deal with that?

If the structure can be that deep, recursion is fragile in Python (default recursion limit ~1000). You can: 1) Increase recursion limit with `sys.setrecursionlimit`, but it risks crashing the interpreter if you go too high due to C stack limits. 2) Rewrite traversal iteratively with an explicit stack (recommended). For enumerating root-to-leaf paths iteratively, you can maintain a stack of frames: (node, child_index). Also maintain a current path list of values. When you go down, append; when you backtrack, pop. Here’s an iterative generator that yields tuples: def iter_paths_iterative(root): stack = [(root, 0)] path = [root.value] # For cycle detection on current path: visiting = {id(root)} node_stack = [root]  # parallel to path for cycle reporting while stack: node, idx = stack[-1] if idx >= len(node.children): # finished this node if len(node.children) == 0: yield tuple(path) stack.pop() visiting.remove(id(node)) path.pop() node_stack.pop() if stack: # increment parent's child index parent, pidx = stack[-1] stack[-1] = (parent, pidx + 1) continue child = node.children[idx] # we do not increment idx here; we increment when we come back or when we decide to skip cid = id(child) if cid in visiting: # construct cycle path # find in node_stack j = next(i for i, n in enumerate(node_stack) if id(n) == cid) cycle = node_stack[j:] + [child] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(n.value for n in cycle)) # descend into child stack.append((child, 0)) visiting.add(cid) path.append(child.value) node_stack.append(child) This yields paths without recursion. It still stores current path of length depth, which is necessary. Note: The “increment parent index” logic is a bit manual. Another style is to store an iterator per node. Would you prefer an iterator-based stack (simpler but a bit less explicit)?
Iterator-based sounds simpler. Could you show that? Also how to handle leaf detection with iterators?

Sure. Use a stack of (node, iterator_over_children). When you descend, push child and its iterator. When a node has no children, its iterator is empty; you can yield immediately, but you also need to backtrack after yielding. One approach: def iter_paths_iter(root): path = [root.value] visiting = {id(root)} node_stack = [root] stack = [(root, iter(root.children))] while stack: node, it = stack[-1] try: child = next(it) except StopIteration: # no more children; if leaf, yield (but only once) if not node.children: yield tuple(path) # backtrack stack.pop() visiting.remove(id(node)) path.pop() node_stack.pop() continue cid = id(child) if cid in visiting: j = next(i for i, n in enumerate(node_stack) if id(n) == cid) cycle = node_stack[j:] + [child] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(n.value for n in cycle)) # descend path.append(child.value) visiting.add(cid) node_stack.append(child) stack.append((child, iter(child.children))) This yields on leaf when backtracking from it. That’s correct because leaf has no children so StopIteration triggers immediately, yields, then backtracks. Caveat: It checks `if not node.children` each time it finishes a node; for non-leaf, it just backtracks. For leaf, it yields once. This avoids recursion depth issues entirely.

I implemented this but I got an issue: for a node with children, it never yields any paths unless the leaf is directly a node with no children. Wait that sounds right. But I’m missing paths sometimes. Could it be because my children is a generator not a list?

Yes. If `children` is a generator/iterator, then: - `iter(node.children)` will consume it. - `if not node.children` won’t work (a generator is truthy even if empty; also you can’t reliably check emptiness without consuming). - Iterating twice over children won’t work. So you need a consistent child container. Options: 1) Materialize children to a list when building nodes. 2) In traversal, treat children as an iterator and detect leaf by whether you were able to pull at least one child. We can avoid `if not node.children` by storing a flag in the stack frame: whether we ever descended from that node. For iterator-based approach: def iter_paths_iter(root): path = [root.value] visiting = {id(root)} node_stack = [root] # frame: (node, iterator, had_child) stack = [(root, iter(root.children), False)] while stack: node, it, had_child = stack[-1] try: child = next(it) # mark that node has at least one child stack[-1] = (node, it, True) except StopIteration: if not had_child: yield tuple(path) stack.pop() visiting.remove(id(node)) path.pop() node_stack.pop() continue cid = id(child) if cid in visiting: j = next(i for i, n in enumerate(node_stack) if id(n) == cid) cycle = node_stack[j:] + [child] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(n.value for n in cycle)) path.append(child.value) visiting.add(cid) node_stack.append(child) stack.append((child, iter(child.children), False)) This works even if children is a generator, as long as each node’s `children` can be iterated once. But beware: if children is computed lazily and depends on external state, repeated traversals might differ. If you can, making children a list is simpler and safer.

In my case children comes from parsing adjacency lists and I store them in a dict mapping id->list, but Node.children property returns an iterator over that list. Something like: class Node: def __init__(self, key, graph): self.key = key self.graph = graph @property def children(self): return (Node(k, self.graph) for k in self.graph[self.key]) So every time I access children I get new Node objects. That might break visited logic right?

Exactly. Each access creates new Node instances, so identity-based cycle detection (`id(node)`) won’t work: you’ll never see the “same” node again by identity, even if it represents the same key. Also your traversal will explode because it creates new Node objects repeatedly. In this model, nodes are logical keys, not stable objects. You should track visited/visiting by `key` (or whatever unique identifier). And for path you should store keys/values, not Node instances. Rewrite traversal to operate on keys: def iter_paths_keys(root_key, graph): path = [root_key] visiting = {root_key} stack = [(root_key, iter(graph.get(root_key, [])), False)] while stack: key, it, had_child = stack[-1] try: child_key = next(it) stack[-1] = (key, it, True) except StopIteration: if not had_child: yield tuple(path) stack.pop() visiting.remove(key) path.pop() continue if child_key in visiting: # cycle path: find index in path j = path.index(child_key) cycle = path[j:] + [child_key] raise CycleError("Cycle detected: " + " -> ".join(cycle)) path.append(child_key) visiting.add(child_key) stack.append((child_key, iter(graph.get(child_key, [])), False)) This avoids creating Node objects at all. If you need Node objects for other reasons, create a canonical Node per key and reuse it (interning), but simplest is to traverse keys.

That’s helpful. I actually do need Node objects because each node has metadata beyond key. But I can store them in a dict by key so I can reuse. How do I best structure that so children returns stable objects?

Use a node registry (cache) mapping key -> Node instance. Children should return those cached instances, not new ones. Example: class Node: def __init__(self, key): self.key = key self.children = []   # list[Node] self.meta = {} def build_nodes(graph, metadata=None): nodes = {k: Node(k) for k in graph} # ensure nodes exist for child-only keys too for k, kids in graph.items(): for c in kids: if c not in nodes: nodes[c] = Node(c) # attach metadata if metadata: for k, meta in metadata.items(): nodes[k].meta = meta # link children for k, kids in graph.items(): nodes[k].children = [nodes[c] for c in kids] return nodes Then traversal can use identity-based cycle detection or key-based; identity is fine because nodes are stable. If graph is large, you can build nodes lazily: `nodes.setdefault(k, Node(k))` during linking.

Okay. Switching gears: I also had a recursion error in a completely different recursive function: I wrote a recursive “flatten” for nested lists, but it crashes on certain inputs. Here’s the function: def flatten(x): if x == []: return [] if isinstance(x[0], list): return flatten(x[0]) + flatten(x[1:]) return [x[0]] + flatten(x[1:]) Why would this overflow?

Two common reasons: 1) Deep nesting or long lists: This implementation recurses once per element plus per nested list level; for a list of length > ~1000 it can overflow even without deep nesting. 2) It assumes `x` is a list and uses slicing `x[1:]`, which creates new lists each call (O(n^2) time/memory). It also breaks if `x` isn’t a list but is an iterable, or if you have self-referential lists (cycles). Example overflow: flatten(list(range(5000))) recurses 5000 deep. Fix: Make it iterative with a stack, or a generator. Iterative version: def flatten_iter(x): out = [] stack = [iter(x)] while stack: try: item = next(stack[-1]) except StopIteration: stack.pop() continue if isinstance(item, list): stack.append(iter(item)) else: out.append(item) return out Generator: def iter_flatten(x): stack = [iter(x)] while stack: try: item = next(stack[-1]) except StopIteration: stack.pop() continue if isinstance(item, list): stack.append(iter(item)) else: yield item Then `list(iter_flatten(x))`. If you need to treat tuples similarly, check for (list, tuple) etc. Also beware strings are iterable; you likely want to treat them as atomic.
I didn’t realize recursion depth could be hit just by list length. That explains it. In my tree case, could I just do sys.setrecursionlimit(10000) and be done?

You can, but it’s risky and not portable. Python recursion uses the C call stack; setting it too high can segfault the interpreter rather than raising RecursionError. Also it doesn’t solve cycles, only depth. For depths around 3000, many environments can handle recursionlimit 10000, but it’s still not ideal. Iterative traversal is safer. If you control the data and can guarantee depth < limit, recursion is fine. If not, iterative is better. If you still want to raise the limit, do it cautiously: import sys sys.setrecursionlimit(10000) But test in the same runtime conditions you’ll deploy in.

Another subtle bug: when I do cycle detection with visiting set of keys, I sometimes get false positives because keys can repeat in different branches but represent distinct entities (same name, different namespace). So key isn’t unique. I do have a unique integer id though. Should I use that?

Yes—cycle detection should use a stable unique identifier for the entity. If “key” is not unique globally, use the unique integer id. If uniqueness is scoped (namespace + name), use a composite key like (namespace, name). False positives happen when your visited/visiting conflates distinct nodes. So store `node.uid` and use that in `visiting`. For cycle error path reporting, store uids in `path` and maybe map uid->display string for messages.
Good. I also want to unit test that my cycle detection works. What are good test cases?

Cover: 1) Simple acyclic chain: A->B->C, yields one path. 2) Branching: A->B, A->C, yields two paths. 3) Shared subtree (DAG): A->B, A->C, B->D, C->D, yields two paths A-B-D and A-C-D, no cycle. 4) Simple cycle: A->B->A should raise CycleError with path containing A->B->A. 5) Self-loop: A->A should raise. 6) Cycle deeper: A->B->C->D->B should raise and show B..B. 7) Large depth without cycle: depth 3000 should not crash if using iterative approach. 8) Non-unique display keys but unique ids: ensure no false positives. Use pytest: import pytest def test_cycle(): graph = {1:[2], 2:[1]} with pytest.raises(CycleError): list(iter_paths_keys(1, graph)) For DAG: graph = {1:[2,3], 2:[4], 3:[4], 4:[]} assert set(iter_paths_keys(1, graph)) == {(1,2,4),(1,3,4)} Adjust for your structures.

I ran into another recursion error in my pretty-printer for the graph. I wrote __repr__ on Node that prints children recursively and it overflows if there’s a cycle. How do people handle repr for potentially cyclic structures?

Avoid recursive full expansion in `__repr__` for graph-like objects. Common patterns: - Keep `__repr__` shallow: show key/id and maybe number of children, not the children themselves. Example: def __repr__(self): return f"Node(id={self.id}, value={self.value!r}, children={len(self.children)})" - Provide a separate method to pretty-print with cycle detection and depth limits, e.g. `to_tree_str(max_depth=...)`. If you really want repr to include children, implement cycle-safe logic using a memo set of visited identities and stop when repeated, e.g. show `...` or `<cycle>`. But Python’s own `repr` for lists/dicts has cycle detection; custom objects don’t get that automatically unless you implement it.

Makes sense. For the tree traversal, I also want postorder instead of preorder for some computations. In recursion it’s easy; iteratively it’s trickier. How do I do postorder iteratively with cycle detection?

For postorder on a tree/graph without cycles (or with cycle detection), you can use a stack with a “state” flag (entered/exited). Classic pattern: def postorder(root): visiting = set() stack_nodes = [] stack = [(root, 0)]  # 0=enter, 1=exit while stack: node, state = stack.pop() nid = id(node) if state == 0: if nid in visiting: # cycle detected along current path # stack_nodes currently is path, but we need maintain it similarly raise CycleError("cycle") visiting.add(nid) stack_nodes.append(node) stack.append((node, 1))  # will process after children # push children enter in reverse so leftmost processed first for child in reversed(node.children): stack.append((child, 0)) else: # exit yield node visiting.remove(nid) stack_nodes.pop() This is postorder for tree semantics. For “all paths” semantics with shared subtrees, this yields nodes multiple times along different paths (because we don’t global-visit). That’s consistent. If you want to compute something bottom-up and avoid recomputing shared subtrees, then you’d treat it as DAG and use a global visited + topological ordering, but that changes semantics. For cycle detection message, keep stack_nodes and on encountering nid in visiting, locate it and report.
In the state machine approach, how do I detect leaf and yield path? I want postorder but still produce root-to-leaf paths maybe at leaf time.

Root-to-leaf paths are naturally generated at leaf “enter” time (preorder) or leaf “exit” time; either works. With the enter/exit stack, maintain a path list. On enter, append; on exit, pop. When entering a node with no children, you can yield the current path snapshot (tuple(path)). That yields each path once. Pseudo: def iter_paths_poststyle(root): path = [] visiting = set() stack_nodes = [] stack = [(root, 0)]  # enter/exit while stack: node, state = stack.pop() nid = id(node) if state == 0: if nid in visiting: # report cycle ... visiting.add(nid) stack_nodes.append(node) path.append(node.value) if not node.children: yield tuple(path) stack.append((node, 1)) for child in reversed(node.children): stack.append((child, 0)) else: visiting.remove(nid) stack_nodes.pop() path.pop() This is basically DFS path enumeration without recursion. If children is an iterator/generator, you can’t do `reversed` or check `not node.children` reliably; you’d need list materialization or frame flags as earlier.

I think I’m set on traversal. But can we go back to my original recursion error? In my real tree traversal recursion, I didn’t have an explicit cycle like d->b. I suspect the cycle comes from my “parent” pointers. Each node has children and also parent, and I accidentally traverse parent too. Here’s a snippet: def walk(node): yield node for child in node.children: yield from walk(child) if node.parent: yield from walk(node.parent) This definitely recurses forever. How do I fix while still sometimes wanting to move up?

That’s no longer a tree traversal; it’s graph traversal on an undirected-ish structure (edges both ways). If you traverse both children and parent without a visited guard or without controlling direction, you’ll bounce between node and parent infinitely. Fix depends on intent: - If you want to traverse entire connected component, use visited set (by id/uid) and traverse neighbors (children + parent) but don’t revisit: def walk(node): visited = set() stack = [node] while stack: n = stack.pop() nid = id(n) if nid in visited: continue visited.add(nid) yield n if n.parent: stack.append(n.parent) stack.extend(reversed(n.children)) - If you want a pure tree traversal downward, don’t traverse parent. - If you want to sometimes move up for specific algorithms (like finding root, computing path to root), don’t do it as part of DFS; do a separate loop: while node.parent: node=node.parent. If you want to traverse but avoid immediate backtracking, you can pass the node you came from (prev) and avoid returning to it: def walk(n, prev=None): yield n for child in n.children: if child is not prev: yield from walk(child, n) if n.parent and n.parent is not prev: yield from walk(n.parent, n) That avoids the immediate 2-cycle, but still can loop if there are larger cycles; visited set is robust.
Great point. I think visited set is safest. But I also need to maintain traversal order: children first, then parent. With iterative stack, order can change. How do I preserve the order?

Use a stack and push neighbors in reverse of desired processing order because stack is LIFO. If desired order is: yield node, then children in order, then parent, then you push parent last (so it’s processed after children) or push in reverse. For a DFS that processes neighbors in a given order, you can: neighbors = list(node.children) if node.parent: neighbors.append(node.parent) Then push reversed(neighbors) to stack so first neighbor processed is children[0]. But with visited, parent might already be visited; that’s fine. If you need strict “children first then parent” relative to each node, DFS with stack will preserve local neighbor priority.
Understood. One last problem: sometimes I see RecursionError but the traceback shows it’s in __getattr__ or property access, not my traversal. Could recursion happen there?

Yes. Common scenarios: - A property calls itself indirectly (e.g., `children` property refers to `self.children` instead of underlying attribute like `_children`). - `__getattr__` tries to access an attribute that triggers `__getattr__` again. - `__repr__` or logging calls properties that call `__repr__`, etc. Example bug: class Node: @property def children(self): return self.children  # infinite recursion Correct: class Node: def __init__(...): self._children = [] @property def children(self): return self._children If traceback points to attribute access, inspect those methods. Add print statements or use debugger to see recursion.
Wow. I actually have: @property def parent(self): return self.parent_map.get(self.id) But parent_map is also a property that uses parent, etc. I might have a loop.

That can definitely recurse. Ensure properties reference underlying fields, not other computed properties that depend back on them, unless carefully designed. Pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, id, parent_map): self.id = id self._parent_map = parent_map @property def parent(self): pid = self._parent_map.get(self.id) return None if pid is None else self._registry[pid] Also avoid naming collisions: if you have both `parent_map` attribute and `@property parent_map`, you can unintentionally call the property inside itself.
