Use underscore-prefixed storage attributes. If you paste the relevant class snippet, we can pinpoint the recursion chain.

Here is a reduced version: class Node: def __init__(self, id, graph): self.id = id self.graph = graph @property def parent_map(self): return self.graph.parent_map  # graph has a parent_map property too @property def parent(self): pid = self.parent_map.get(self.id) if pid is None: return None return self.graph.get_node(pid) class Graph: def __init__(self, edges): self.edges = edges @property def parent_map(self): # builds it on demand pm = {} for p, kids in self.edges.items(): for k in kids: pm[k] = p return pm def get_node(self, id): return Node(id, self) I see recursion sometimes when I call node.parent repeatedly.

The immediate code doesn’t have direct recursion, but it has a performance pitfall: `Graph.parent_map` rebuilds the entire map every access. If `node.parent` calls `self.parent_map`, that rebuilds each time. That won’t cause recursion, but can be extremely slow. RecursionError might come from something else interacting (like repr printing parent which prints parent, etc.). However, there is a subtle recursion possibility: if `Graph.parent_map` or `Graph.edges` property in real code references nodes/parents and triggers `node.parent` again, then you could get recursion. Also, `Graph.get_node` creates a new Node each time, so identity-based cycle detection won’t work (same issue as before). For parent traversal, repeatedly calling node.parent creates a fresh Node each step; that’s fine logically but can be confusing. Fixes: 1) Cache parent_map: class Graph: def __init__(...): self._parent_map = None @property def parent_map(self): if self._parent_map is None: pm = {} ... self._parent_map = pm return self._parent_map 2) Cache nodes: class Graph: def __init__(...): self._nodes = {} def get_node(self, id): if id not in self._nodes: self._nodes[id] = Node(id, self) return self._nodes[id] Now nodes are stable and parent_map is not rebuilt. If RecursionError persists, check `__repr__` / logging. For instance, if Node.__repr__ includes parent, calling repr(node) calls repr(parent) calls repr(grandparent) etc. That can overflow for long chains.
That’s it! My __repr__ does: def __repr__(self): return f"Node(id={self.id}, parent={self.parent})" So printing a node prints its parent which prints its parent...

Exactly. That’s recursion through repr, not traversal. Fix by making repr shallow: def __repr__(self): pid = self.parent_map.get(self.id) return f"Node(id={self.id}, parent_id={pid})" Or just omit parent: return f"Node(id={self.id})" If you want detailed printing, implement a separate method with a depth limit: def repr_chain(self, limit=10): parts = [] n = self for _ in range(limit): parts.append(str(n.id)) n = n.parent if n is None: break else: parts.append("...") return " <- ".join(parts) This avoids infinite recursion and is explicit.
Perfect. I think I’ve got a handle on all the recursion error sources now. Could you summarize the key takeaways so I don’t repeat these mistakes?

- RecursionError often means: cycle in data, missing/incorrect base case, or depth > recursion limit. - Tree traversals assume acyclic; if cycles possible, add cycle detection (`visiting` set) and optionally raise with path info. - If you need “all paths,” don’t use a global `visited` that suppresses revisits; use only recursion-stack detection. - Avoid mutable default args (`path=[]`) and avoid storing references to a mutable path; copy or use tuples when yielding. - For deep structures, prefer iterative traversal with explicit stack to avoid recursion limits. - If nodes are re-created on the fly, identity-based visited fails; use stable unique ids or intern/caching. - Be careful with properties/`__getattr__` and `__repr__`: they can cause recursion indirectly (repr(parent) -> repr(parent)...). Keep `__repr__` shallow and cycle-safe. - Don’t rebuild expensive derived structures (like parent_map) on every access; cache. If you share a bit more about your target output format (list of paths, streaming, cycle error message requirements), I can help shape the final traversal API.
I want to use pdb more effectively when these recursion-ish bugs happen. I usually just run and stare at the traceback. How would you debug something like the __repr__ recursion with pdb?

For recursion triggered by printing/logging, pdb is useful if you avoid triggering `__repr__` again inside the debugger. A practical flow: 1) Reproduce with a minimal snippet that triggers the recursion, but don’t print the object directly. Instead call the method you suspect. 2) Set a breakpoint inside `__repr__`: import pdb class Node: def __repr__(self): pdb.set_trace() return f"Node(id={self.id}, parent={self.parent})" Then do something that implicitly calls repr, like `print(node)` or `f"{node}"`. 3) In pdb, don’t type `p self` or `p node` because that calls `repr(self)` again and recurses. Use `id(self)`, or inspect attributes that don’t call repr: (Pdb) p self.id (Pdb) p type(self) (Pdb) p self.__dict__   # safe if values’ repr is safe; can still be risky 4) Use `where` (or `bt`) to see the call stack and confirm it’s repr calling parent calling repr. 5) Step through carefully with `n` (next) / `s` (step), and inspect `self.parent` by looking at the id only, e.g. compute parent id without constructing parent object. If `self.parent` is a property that constructs nodes, you can inspect intermediate computations: (Pdb) p self.parent_map.get(self.id) That avoids calling `self.parent` and avoids a new Node and repr chain. If the recursion is deep and pdb breaks late, you can set a breakpoint earlier using `break Node.__repr__` and run under `python -m pdb yourscript.py`.
When I’m in pdb and I do `pp some_list_of_nodes`, it sometimes hangs or errors because repr is too big. Is there a way to print safely?

Yes. A few approaches: 1) Print only ids/keys with a comprehension that avoids repr of the Node objects: (Pdb) p [n.id for n in nodes] 2) Use `len()` and slicing: (Pdb) p len(nodes) (Pdb) p [n.id for n in nodes[:10]] 3) If you need to see structure, write a helper function that returns safe primitives: def safe_node(n): return {"id": n.id, "child_count": len(n.children)} Then in pdb: (Pdb) p [safe_node(n) for n in nodes[:5]] 4) In Python 3.11+, `breakpoint()` drops you into pdb; you can also use the `reprlib` module to limit output: import reprlib reprlib.repr(nodes)  # shortens long containers, but still calls element repr It still calls each element’s repr, so it only helps if element repr is safe. If element repr is risky, always map to primitives first.
For recursion errors, is there a way to see the repeating pattern without printing the entire huge traceback?

Two tactics: 1) Use `faulthandler` to dump a traceback on demand or on a timeout, and it tends to be more compact/consistent: import faulthandler, signal faulthandler.register(signal.SIGUSR1)  # on Unix Then send SIGUSR1 to the process to dump stack. For RecursionError specifically, you still get a Python traceback, but you can reduce noise by catching it and printing a trimmed stack summary: import traceback, sys try: ... except RecursionError: tb = traceback.extract_tb(sys.exc_info()[2]) # Show last N frames for frame in tb[-30:]: print(frame) 2) Programmatically detect recursion loops by logging a small signature of the call path. For example, in a suspect property: import inspect print(inspect.stack()[0].function)  # expensive; use sparingly Better: log a counter and key values, and stop after some number: self._debug_count += 1 if self._debug_count < 20: print("parent called", self.id) This gives you the repeating sequence.
Can you show me how to use sys.setprofile or sys.settrace to catch recursion patterns? I’ve never used them.

`sys.settrace` lets you intercept every function call/return/line, which is heavy but can be useful for short reproductions. Example: detect repeated calls to Node.__repr__ and Node.parent: import sys from collections import Counter counts = Counter() def tracer(frame, event, arg): if event == "call": code = frame.f_code name = code.co_name qual = name # If you want filename + line: # qual = f"{code.co_filename}:{code.co_firstlineno}:{name}" if name in ("__repr__", "parent", "parent_map"): counts[name] += 1 if counts[name] <= 20: # avoid printing self repr; use locals carefully loc = frame.f_locals if "self" in loc and hasattr(loc["self"], "id"): print(f"call {name} self.id={loc['self'].id}") else: print(f"call {name}") if counts[name] > 2000: raise RuntimeError("Stopping trace; looks recursive") return tracer sys.settrace(tracer) try: print(node)  # triggers recursion finally: sys.settrace(None) This prints the repeating pattern and stops early. Caveats: - Very slow: it traces everything in Python. - Avoid accessing attributes that trigger properties/repr again. - Use only in small reproductions. `sys.setprofile` is similar but only tracks calls/returns (not lines), less overhead.

Nice. Another debugging question: for the cycle detection in graph traversal, I want the error message to show not just ids but also some metadata (like name). But I’ve had cases where building that message triggers more property access and causes issues. How do I safely format debug info?

Treat error reporting as potentially dangerous: it may touch properties that compute lazily, do I/O, or recurse. Safe patterns: 1) Store the display fields you need as plain attributes (no properties) at construction time, e.g. `node.name` as a real string, not computed. 2) In the cycle detection, extract only safe primitives that you know won’t recurse. For example, use `node.id` and `node.name` if those are plain attributes. 3) If metadata is expensive or property-based, fetch it once and cache it in a safe attribute, or provide a safe method that never traverses: def debug_label(node): # only use direct attributes or dict lookups return f"{node.id}:{getattr(node, '_name', '?')}" 4) Avoid including `node` itself in f-strings (`{node}`) because that calls `__format__`/`__str__`/`__repr__`. So instead of: raise CycleError(f"Cycle: {cycle_nodes}") Do: labels = [debug_label(n) for n in cycle_nodes] raise CycleError("Cycle: " + " -> ".join(labels))
In my traversal, I sometimes want to keep going and collect all cycles rather than raising on the first. Is that doable without exponential blowups?

Collecting “all cycles” in a general directed graph can be expensive (there can be exponentially many simple cycles). But you can collect “cycle occurrences encountered during DFS from this root following this traversal order” without aiming for completeness. If your aim is: “while enumerating paths, record any back-edge cycles encountered and continue,” you can do that by catching the cycle, recording it, and then skipping that edge. In an iterative DFS over adjacency lists keyed by uid: def iter_paths_collect_cycles(root, graph): path = [root] visiting = {root} cycles = []  # list[list[uid]] stack = [(root, iter(graph.get(root, [])), False)] while stack: node, it, had_child = stack[-1] try: child = next(it) stack[-1] = (node, it, True) except StopIteration: if not had_child: yield tuple(path) stack.pop() visiting.remove(node) path.pop() continue if child in visiting: j = path.index(child) cycles.append(path[j:] + [child]) # skip descending into child; continue with next child continue path.append(child) visiting.add(child) stack.append((child, iter(graph.get(child, [])), False)) return cycles But note: returning `cycles` from a generator doesn’t work directly (it becomes StopIteration.value). Better: pass in a list to fill, or yield cycles separately, or collect in an object. A clean API is to separate concerns: - One function to validate (collect cycles). - Another to enumerate paths assuming validated acyclic. For “not exponential”: if your graph is cyclic, the number of back-edges encountered is bounded by edges explored in your DFS (O(E) for a fixed traversal), so collecting those cycle snippets is manageable.
Can we talk about the “mutable default argument” bug again? I understand it in theory, but I still accidentally write it. Any practical advice to catch it early?

Practical safeguards: 1) Make it a rule: never use `[]`, `{}`, or `set()` as a default. Use `None` and create inside. 2) Linters catch it: - Ruff: `B006` / `B008` style warnings (depending config). - Flake8-bugbear flags mutable defaults. - Pylint warns too. 3) In code review, search for `def .*=\[` or `def .*=\{`. 4) If you must use a default container for performance, use an immutable like `()` and convert, or use `None` with a cached sentinel that you never mutate. Example good pattern: _sentinel = object() def f(x=_sentinel): if x is _sentinel: x = [] ... But usually `None` is enough.
I also had a bug where I appended to a list while iterating over it in traversal, which caused weird behavior and sometimes infinite loops. How can that happen?

If you iterate directly over a list and mutate it (append) during iteration, Python’s list iterator will see the new elements too, so the loop may never terminate if you keep appending. Example: for child in node.children: node.children.append(new_child)  # dangerous In graph-building, this can accidentally create self-growing adjacency lists. Fix: either: - Iterate over a snapshot: `for child in list(node.children): ...` - Or build a separate list of additions, then extend after iteration. - Or ensure traversal and mutation are separated phases (build graph fully, then traverse read-only). If the mutation is intended (like BFS), use a deque/queue and manage indices explicitly.
Good call. For deep debugging, do you recommend using the `logging` module or print statements? Logging sometimes triggers repr too.

Logging is better for real projects, but you’re right: logging arguments can trigger repr/str. Use structured logging style that avoids formatting objects eagerly. In logging, prefer parameterized messages: logger.debug("visiting node_id=%s depth=%d", node.id, depth) This defers formatting and avoids calling `repr(node)`. Avoid: logger.debug(f"visiting {node}")  # calls __format__/__str__ immediately Also consider adding safe fields only (ids, counts), and add guards for very verbose logs. For quick local debugging, `print(node.id)` is often safer than `print(node)`.
One more: I increased recursionlimit and then Python crashed with a segfault in CI. That was scary. Why does that happen instead of a clean RecursionError?

`RecursionError` is raised when Python detects recursion depth exceeding its limit, but the actual call stack is the C stack. If you set the recursion limit too high, Python may keep recursing until the C stack overflows, at which point you can segfault before Python can raise an exception. This is environment-dependent: - Different OS / architecture / stack size limits. - CI often has smaller stack limits. So raising recursionlimit is not a robust fix. It can be acceptable for small increases with controlled inputs, but for potentially deep recursion, prefer iterative algorithms.
Makes sense. I think I’ll refactor most of my traversals to iterative. If I send you my final API idea, can you sanity-check it?

Yes—paste the proposed functions/classes and a couple example usages, plus constraints: - Do you need to enumerate paths, traverse nodes, compute aggregates? - Do you want cycle detection strict (raise) or permissive (record/skip)? - What identifiers are unique (uid vs name)? - Are children lists or iterators? Then I can check correctness, edge cases (DAG sharing, generators, mutation), and suggest small improvements for clarity and safety.
